root_note: from denigrare (to blacken, defame) from niger (black)

d: showing or having skill with one's hands or mind
example: The surgeon's dexterous technique allowed her to complete the procedure in half the expected time.

d: a person who cultivates an area of interest without real commitment or knowledge
example: His critics dismissed him as a dilettante, lacking the expertise to make the claims he advanced.
root_note: from dilettare (to delight) from Latin delectare

d: not candid or sincere; pretending to know less than one does
example: The apology was widely viewed as disingenuous given that the same behavior had continued after it was issued.

d: to regard or represent as being of little worth; to belittle
example: She was careful never to disparage competing research, even when it contradicted her own findings.
root_note: from disparager (to marry unequally) — to treat as inferior

d: to conceal or disguise one's true feelings or motives
example: The witness struggled to dissemble under cross-examination, and the inconsistencies became apparent.
root_note: from dissimuler (to disguise) from Latin dissimulare

d: ready to accept control or instruction; submissive
example: The usually docile committee pushed back sharply on the proposed budget cuts.
root_note: from docilis (easily taught) from docere (to teach)

d: deceitfulness; saying one thing while meaning another
example: The investigation revealed a pattern of duplicity that stretched across multiple departments.

d: expressing gratitude or approval in an unrestrained way; gushing
example: The effusive praise in the blurb was difficult to reconcile with the reviewer's private reservations.

d: to make something more attractive by adding decorative details; to exaggerate
example: The account had been embellished over the years until it bore little resemblance to the original event.
root_note: from embellir (to make beautiful) from bel (beautiful)

d: to mention a number of things one by one
example: The report enumerated seven distinct failures in the oversight process.
root_note: from enumerare (to count out) from numerus (number)

d: a person or thing that is a perfect example of a quality
example: The building became the epitome of sustainable design when it won every major architectural award.
root_note: from epitome (an abridgment) from epitemnein (to cut short, summarize)

d: tending to avoid commitment or direct answers
example: The evasive response confirmed reporters' suspicion that the agency had something to hide.
root_note: from evadere (to go out, escape) from e + vadere (to go)

d: to irritate intensely; to frustrate greatly
example: The repeated delays exasperated the community members who had waited years for action.
root_note: from exasperare (to roughen, irritate) from asper (rough)

d: unreasonably high in price or amount

root_note: from exorbitare (to deviate) from ex + orbita (track, course)

d: filled with lively energy and enthusiasm
example: The exuberant crowd response at the debut performance exceeded the producer's expectations.
root_note: from exuberare (to be fruitful, overflow) from uber (fertile)

d: to pretend to feel or be affected by something
example: She feigned indifference to the outcome even as her team watched the results come in.
root_note: from feindre (to pretend) from Latin fingere (to shape, invent)

d: intense and passionate feeling
example: The fervor of the early supporters sustained the campaign through months of low polling numbers.

d: to instigate or stir up trouble or discontent

root_note: from fomentare (to apply warm lotions) — later, to stir up

d: uncalled for; lacking good reason; done without payment
example: The gratuitous detail in the report added length without adding clarity.
root_note: from gratuitus (freely given, without return) from gratis (freely)

d: lacking freshness through overuse; trite and unoriginal
example: The hackneyed metaphors throughout the speech suggested the writer had not revised it in years.
root_note: from haquenée (horse for hire) — overused and worn out

d: arrogantly superior and disdainful
example: The haughty dismissal of the junior researcher's findings proved to be a costly error.

d: the practice of claiming higher standards than one actually follows
example: Voters are quick to punish perceived hypocrisy when a politician's actions contradict their public positions.
root_note: from hypokrisis (acting a part) from hypokrinesthai (to play a part)

d: not showing proper respect; bold and disrespectful
example: The impudent response from the subordinate surprised even those who had witnessed the dispute.
root_note: from impudens (shameless) from in + pudere (to feel shame)

d: not resulting from or achieved through deliberate planning; accidental
example: The inadvertent disclosure of the data triggered a mandatory breach notification.
root_note: from in + advertere (to turn toward, pay attention)

d: to encourage or stir up violent or unlawful behavior
example: The prosecution argued that the speech was designed to incite the crowd to action.
root_note: from incitare (to urge forward) from in + citare (to set in motion)

d: done at random without careful judgment
example: The indiscriminate application of the policy caused harm in cases it was never meant to address.

d: having or showing no skill; clumsy and incompetent
example: The inept handling of the media inquiry turned a manageable story into a crisis.
root_note: from ineptus (unsuitable, absurd) from in + aptus (fitting)

d: not harmful or offensive; not likely to cause controversy
example: What seemed like an innocuous question turned out to be a careful test of the candidate's integrity.
root_note: from innocuus (harmless) from in + nocere (to hurt)

d: lacking flavor, vigor, or interest; dull
example: The insipid prose of the first draft was transformed by a skilled editor into something genuinely readable.
root_note: from insipidus (tasteless) from in + sapere (to taste, know)

d: showing a rude and arrogant lack of respect
example: The insolent tone of the letter prompted the recipient to escalate the matter to legal counsel.
root_note: from insolens (unusual, excessive) from in + solere (to be accustomed)

d: excessively inclined to go to law; involving or prone to litigation
example: The litigious history of the company made potential partners reluctant to enter into agreements without extensive legal review.

d: having or showing a wish to do evil to others
example: The malevolent intent behind the campaign became clear only after the damage had been done.
root_note: from malevolens (wishing evil) from male (badly) + volens (wishing)

d: deserving reward or praise; having merit
example: The committee recognized the meritorious work of the team with a special commendation.
root_note: from meritorius (serving for pay) from meritum (merit)

d: to appease someone's anger or anxiety; to soften

root_note: from mollificare (to make soft) from mollis (soft)

d: more generous than is usual or necessary

root_note: from munificus (generous) from munus (gift) + facere (to make)

d: failing to take proper care in doing something
example: The contractor was found negligent for failing to inspect the structure before approving it for occupancy.

d: harmful, poisonous, or very unpleasant
example: The noxious fumes from the facility prompted residents to call for an immediate shutdown.

d: to make something unclear or confusing
example: The dense technical language seemed designed to obfuscate rather than explain.
root_note: from obfuscare (to darken) from ob + fuscus (dark)

d: involving heavy obligation or difficulty; burdensome
example: The onerous reporting requirements deterred small organizations from applying for the grant.

d: not able to be seen through; difficult to understand
example: The policy document was so opaque that even specialists struggled to determine its practical implications.

d: designed to impress or attract notice; showy
example: The ostentatious redesign of the headquarters was widely criticized as an inappropriate use of public funds.
root_note: from ostentare (to show off) from ostendere (to show)

d: the presence of something in only very small or insufficient amounts
example: The paucity of evidence made it impossible to draw firm conclusions from the study.

d: to make someone less angry or upset; to pacify
example: The statement was an attempt to placate shareholders ahead of the annual general meeting.

d: slow and heavy; laboriously dull or tedious
example: The ponderous delivery of the keynote address lost the audience well before the halfway point.

d: to be a sign or warning that something is likely to happen
example: The sharp drop in orders portended a difficult quarter for the manufacturer.
root_note: from portendere (to indicate, foretell) from por + tendere (to stretch)

d: having developed abilities earlier than usual
example: The precocious researcher had published her first paper before finishing her undergraduate degree.
root_note: from praecox (early ripening) from prae + coquere (to cook, ripen)

d: attempting to impress by assuming greater importance than actually possessed
example: The pretentious title of the report obscured what was actually a straightforward analysis.
root_note: from praetendere (to stretch before, allege as an excuse)

d: to put an end to something by force; to suppress or calm
example: The swift response was enough to quell speculation before it gained momentum in the press.
root_note: from cwellan (to kill, put to death)

d: exceedingly idealistic; unrealistic and impractical
example: The quixotic plan to eliminate homelessness within a year was admirable but not achievable.

d: to revoke, cancel, or repeal a law, order, or agreement
example: The board voted to rescind the decision after new information came to light.
root_note: from rescindere (to cut off, annul) from re + scindere (to cut)

d: to think deeply about something over a long time
example: She spent the following weeks ruminating on the implications before committing to a response.
root_note: from ruminare (to chew the cud) — to turn something over in the mind

d: a split or division between strongly opposed groups
example: The policy debate deepened the schism between the party's progressive and moderate wings.
root_note: from schisma (cleft, division) from schizein (to split)

d: formal and dignified; serious and not cheerful
example: The solemn ceremony reflected the gravity of what the institution was commemorating.

d: difficult to understand; obscure in meaning
example: The abstruse theoretical framework in the paper's first section deterred many readers from reaching its conclusions.
root_note: from abstrusus (concealed, obscure) from abstrudere (to thrust away)

d: an award or expression of praise or admiration
example: The accolade was well deserved, but the researcher noted that many colleagues had contributed to the finding.
root_note: from accolade (an embrace) from Latin collum (neck) — originally bestowed by embrace

d: the act of making something bad or unsatisfactory better
example: The report documented the slow amelioration of conditions over the five-year program period.

d: giving a good impression of future success; favorable
example: The auspicious opening day sales suggested the product had found the audience the team had hoped for.
root_note: from auspicium (omen, divination) from avis (bird) + specere (to look)

d: a cause of great distress or annoyance; something that ruins
example: Regulatory ambiguity remained the bane of everyone trying to operate within the sector.
root_note: from bana (slayer, murderer) — something that destroys

d: to express grief or regret about something
example: Analysts bemoaned the lack of transparency in the quarterly figures.

d: gentle and kindly; not harmful; not malignant
example: What appeared to be a benign administrative oversight turned out to have significant consequences.
root_note: from benignus (kind, generous) from bene (well) + gignere (to produce)

d: showing a casual lack of concern; carefree
example: Her blithe disregard for the risks alarmed colleagues who understood what was at stake.

d: to support or strengthen; to make something firmer
example: The new data bolstered the case for a policy reversal that had previously seemed politically impossible.
root_note: from bolster (a long pillow) — to prop up

d: involving or causing great damage or misery; disastrous
example: The calamitous mismanagement of the project set the entire program back by three years.

d: severely critical of others; fault-finding
example: The censorious review was dismissed by the author as motivated by rivalry rather than honest assessment.
root_note: from censorius (of a censor) from censor (judge of morals)

d: to hold together and form a unified whole; to be logically consistent
example: The various sections of the report did not cohere into a single coherent argument.
root_note: from cohaerere (to stick together) from co + haerere (to stick)

d: willing to please; showing eager willingness to oblige
example: The complaisant staff absorbed every new request without complaint, even as workloads became untenable.
root_note: from complaisant (pleasing) from Latin complacere (to please greatly)

d: extremely severe or cruel, especially regarding laws or measures
example: The draconian penalties for minor infractions were widely seen as disproportionate to the harm caused.
root_note: from Draco, Athenian lawmaker notorious for harsh punishments

d: cheerful and full of energy; enthusiastically confident
example: Her ebullient presentation style made even dry subject matter engaging for the audience.
root_note: from ebullire (to bubble up, boil over)

d: a speech or piece of writing that praises someone or something highly
example: The encomium delivered at the retirement dinner was heartfelt but perhaps too lavish for the occasion.
root_note: from enkomion (praise) from en + komos (revel, celebration)

d: to use ambiguous language to conceal the truth or avoid commitment
example: When asked directly, the candidate equivocated rather than stating a clear position.
root_note: from aequivocus (equal voice, ambiguous) from aequus + vox (voice)

d: spoken or done without preparation; improvised
example: The most impressive part of her performance was the extemporaneous closing that drew on the morning's discussion.
root_note: from ex tempore (out of the moment, on the spot)

d: unfortunate and deserving of pity; unlucky
example: The hapless intern had been assigned to manage a project that three senior employees had already abandoned.
root_note: from hap (luck, fortune) + less — without luck

d: a forceful, lengthy speech criticizing someone or urging action
example: The harangue lasted forty minutes and left the audience exhausted before the main session had begun.
root_note: from harangue (a public speech) from Old Italian aringo

d: the quality of being disrespectfully bold
example: The sheer impudence of the counteroffer suggested the other side was not negotiating in good faith.

d: designed to cause fires; or tending to stir up conflict
example: The incendiary editorial provoked immediate threats of legal action from those it had named.
root_note: from incendium (fire) from incendere (to set on fire)

d: ignorant of or uninterested in cultures or peoples beyond one's own; narrow-minded
example: The insular hiring practices of the firm had produced a leadership team with almost no diversity of background.
root_note: from insularis (of an island) from insula (island)

d: to make someone feel frightened or overawed to prevent them from acting
example: The aim of the tactic was to intimidate potential whistleblowers before they could come forward.

d: insulting, abusive, or violent language used to attack or criticize
example: The letter descended into invective rather than argument, which undermined its intended effect.
root_note: from invehi (to attack with words) from in + vehere (to carry)

d: to praise highly
example: Critics lauded the debut novel as the most assured first fiction published that year.

d: lacking energy; affected by lethargy
example: The lethargic pace of recovery disappointed those who had expected faster results from the intervention.

d: lacking energy or enthusiasm; lethargic
example: The team appeared listless in the second half, unable to generate the momentum that had characterized the opening.
root_note: from lust (pleasure, desire) + less — without desire or energy

d: bright or shining; clear and easy to understand
example: The luminous quality of the glass installation changed with every shift in the natural light.
root_note: from luminosus (full of light) from lumen (light)

d: a deep feeling of sadness, often without obvious cause
example: The novel's closing pages were suffused with a melancholy that readers found both moving and unresolved.
root_note: from melankholia from melas (black) + khole (bile) — black bile, one of four humors

d: extremely small; tiny
example: The difference between the two readings was minuscule but statistically significant.

d: a wrong or inaccurate name or description
example: Calling it a 'free market' was a misnomer given the extensive subsidies supporting the industry.
root_note: from mes (wrongly) + nomer (to name) from Latin nominare

d: dull, tedious, and repetitious; lacking in variety
example: The monotonous cadence of the presentation caused several audience members to lose focus.
root_note: from monotonos (of one tone) from monos (single) + tonos (tone)

d: rejecting all moral principles; believing life is meaningless
example: The nihilistic streak in the film was balanced by a final scene of unexpected tenderness.
root_note: from nihil (nothing) — the belief that nothing has meaning

d: stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion; doggedly persistent
example: The obstinate refusal to reconsider prevented what could have been a straightforward resolution.
root_note: from obstinatus (resolute, stubborn) from obstare (to stand against)

d: asserting authority or interfering in an intrusive way; busybody
example: The officious junior administrator created more problems than he solved by inserting himself into every decision.

d: ostentatiously rich and luxurious; wealthy
example: The opulent headquarters stood in sharp contrast to the austerity measures being imposed on staff.

d: elaborately decorated; highly elaborate in style
example: The ornate language of the original document required considerable simplification for public communication.
root_note: from ornatus (adorned) from ornare (to adorn, equip)

d: a solution or remedy for all difficulties or diseases
example: Technology was presented as a panacea, but the underlying social factors remained unaddressed.

d: a person or thing regarded as a perfect example of a quality
example: The institution had long regarded itself as a paragon of integrity, which made the scandal especially damaging.
root_note: from paragone (touchstone, model) from Greek parakonan (to sharpen)

d: to cause something to happen suddenly or prematurely
example: The abrupt resignation precipitated a leadership crisis that took months to resolve.
root_note: from praecipitare (to throw headlong) from praeceps (headlong)

d: remarkably great in extent, size, or degree
example: The prodigious output of the team during the final sprint surprised everyone, including the project leads.
root_note: from prodigiosus (marvellous, unnatural) from prodigium (omen, wonder)

d: recklessly extravagant or wasteful; licentious
example: The profligate spending of the early years left the organization without reserves for the downturn.
root_note: from profligatus (ruined, dissolute) from profligare (to ruin)

d: to spread and promote widely; to reproduce and multiply
example: Social media made it easier to propagate misinformation faster than corrections could follow.
root_note: from propagare (to spread, extend) from propago (offshoot, layer)

d: to express sharp disapproval or criticism because of behavior
example: The senior official rebuked the department for bypassing standard procedures without authorization.
root_note: from rebukier (to cut back, restrain) — to cut back sharply

d: to withdraw and say that a former belief was wrong
example: Under pressure, the witness recanted his earlier testimony, citing fear as the reason he had misled the court.
root_note: from recantare (to recall, revoke) from re + cantare (to sing)

d: not known by many; obscure and difficult to understand
example: The essay drew on recondite sources that even specialists in the field had not encountered.
root_note: from reconditus (hidden away) from recondere (to put away, conceal)

d: to express disapproval or disappointment to someone
example: She reproached the committee for approving a budget it had not reviewed in full.
root_note: from reprocher (to bring near, blame) from Latin prope (near)

d: to feel deep regret or sorrow for something
example: He came to rue the hasty decision that had been made without adequate information.

d: having enough money to meet financial obligations; able to dissolve other substances
example: Keeping the organization solvent through the downturn required difficult decisions that the board had delayed too long.
root_note: from solvere (to loosen, pay) from se + luere (to release)

d: to express opinions loudly and forcefully; to exclaim noisily
example: Several members vociferated their objections before the chairperson could restore order.
root_note: from vociferari (to cry aloud) from vox (voice) + ferre (to carry)
